text: The final chamber. An elderly lich sits at a desk buried in parchment. 'Adventurer,' it rasps, 'do you know ANYTHING about estate taxes? I've been dead for 300 years and they keep sending letters.'

success.text: Three hours later, you've found a deduction for 'phylactery depreciation.' The lich weeps dust and pays you from its hoard.

fail.text: You make it worse. The lich now owes back taxes in two kingdoms, one of which no longer exists. It curses you out of pure administrative grief.

success.text: The lich crumbles, almost relieved. Its final words: 'tell them... I'm... exempt.' The hoard is yours, minus filing fees.

fail.text: The lich hits you with a sheaf of parchment. It's the deadliest thing in the room and it knows it.

choices.text: Suggest it just stop opening the letters

outcomes.text: The lich freezes mid-shuffle. 'You carry a third-circle curse AND you dispense wisdom? Professional courtesy demands an exchange.' It plucks the curse off you like lint and files it under MISC.
The lich stares at you. Three centuries of dread dissolve. 'I can... simply not?' It hands you gold with trembling claws. You've slain the real monster: obligation.

text: The final door opens onto a vault. Actual treasure, actually glittering. A plaque reads: 'CONGRATULATIONS. PLEASE REVIEW YOUR EXPERIENCE ON THE WAY OUT.'

outcomes.text: Coins, trinkets, a thing that might be jewelry or dentistry. A clean haul.
The floor was a scale. The exit fee is calculated by weight. Still profitable, barely.

choices.text: Take only what fits in one hand

success.text: Restraint! The vault's guardian, a tired spectral accountant, waves you through and rounds your haul UP for politeness.

fail.text: Your one hand cramps around the single heaviest object. You leave with it and a limp.

outcomes.text: You write 'atmospheric, good bones, gap unmarked' on the guest ledger. A small stipend of appreciation materializes. The dungeon has pride.

text: The last chamber holds no monster. It holds a janitor, mopping around the bones. 'Mind the wet bones,' he says. 'You here for the hoard? It's behind the mop closet. I don't make the rules but I do clean up after them.'

choices.text: Ask him to open the closet

success.text: He shrugs and unlocks it. 'Take the shiny ones. The cursed ones hum. You'll figure it out.' Solid professional advice.

fail.text: 'Closet's for staff.' He mops you gently but firmly toward the exit. You grab a souvenir from the lost-and-found on the way out.

choices.text: Fight him. He's clearly the boss

outcomes.text: He deflects your entire offense with the mop. Decades of technique. You wake up outside, neatly propped against a rock, with a pity coin in your palm.
You were right. He IS the boss. But so many years of mopping has left him mostly retired, and he yields the closet with grace and one devastating shoulder throw.
You mop. He talks. Three hours of dungeon gossip and a fair split of whatever rolls out from under the bones. Honest work.

text: The final chamber is... a gift shop. Shelves of branded merchandise: tiny plush mimics, 'I SURVIVED {site}' mugs, novelty curse scrolls. A skeleton in a lanyard waves. 'Exit's through here. Everyone exits through here.'

outcomes.text: The plush mimic is soft, judgmental, and possibly load-bearing to your happiness now.
The mug is real. The curse scroll was also, it turns out, real. Complimentary with purchase.

choices.text: Ask what happened to the actual boss

outcomes.text: 'Franchised,' says the skeleton, adjusting its lanyard. 'The margins on terror are awful. Retail is forever.' It comps you a keychain for asking.

success.text: You palm a snow globe of the dungeon itself. Inside it, a tiny you palms a tinier snow globe. Best not to think about it.

fail.text: The plush mimics were real mimics. Of course they were. Store security is impeccable and has teeth.
